ops.body:
# Kill authority

A PID alone is never kill authority. A Record carries PID + StartTime + Comm + Generation, and Reap revalidates ALL FOUR against the live process table before it signals: PID must exist, start time AND comm must match, and the generation must differ from the reaper's own. Any mismatch drops the stale record without signaling; any probe error is Undetermined and touches nothing (fail closed). StartTime is the prober's platform-native opaque form (darwin: the start Timeval via sysctl kern.proc.pid; linux: /proc start ticks) compared as an opaque string.

Ordering: a daemon builds a Reaper with a fresh unique Generation and runs one Reap at cold start, BEFORE accepting registrations, so a predecessor's orphans are cleared before the successor rebinds their sockets. Track snapshots a fresh child's identity through the SAME prober Reap revalidates with, guaranteeing stored start time and comm match a future probe exactly.

# The reap ladder (reapOrphan)

SIGTERM → grace → re-revalidate → SIGKILL. ESRCH at any point is success ("already gone"). Never escalate to SIGKILL once the PID has been reused or exec'd away during the grace window — no longer provably ours. Undetermined probe or signal errors fail closed (keep the record, retry next pass). reapOne verdicts: recorded process gone → drop stale record; pid reused → drop; own current-generation child → never signal, keep; Undetermined → keep. Never signal init or self.

# Process groups (v2 Pool workers, 7623022)

TrackGroup records a child whose PID leads its own process group AND a dedicated session created for it. Record.ProcessGroup means PID is also the pgid and signals target the ENTIRE group — only after the group's dedicated session membership has been revalidated; Record.SessionID is that dedicated session's id, which remains the group's durable kernel identity after the leader exits, so a leaderless group is still identity-gated through the session rather than the dead pid. Reap thus revalidates "process OR dedicated-session identity" before signaling. Untrack removes a synchronously reaped child's record (supervise.Pool's normal path); DefaultReapSettlement bounds post-SIGKILL identity polling so settlement is proven, not assumed.

# Platform probes

- darwin: sysctl kern.proc.pid; empty result = gone (errNoProc), any sysctl failure = genuine probe error (Undetermined).
- linux: /proc/<pid>/stat. comm is field 2, parenthesized and free to contain spaces or ')' — split on the LAST ')'; starttime is field 22, i.e. index 19 of the post-comm fields.

# Identity + boot sessions

probe.Identity: {PID, StartTime} is the only safe kill authority (a reused PID gets a fresh StartTime; within one boot, start stamps are monotonic across creations). Identity.Boot carries BootID (darwin: kernel boot time secs+usecs; linux: per-boot random UUID) because linux start stamps are only unique within one boot — a recorded identity whose Boot differs cannot have survived and reads as dead.

# FileStore

Records live in one JSON file guarded by a proc.Flock lock file so concurrent daemons serialize read-modify-writes (a spawning daemon's Add never races a successor's Remove); writes are atomic (temp+rename); missing file = empty set; records match on PID+StartTime.
